reasoning.A:
DECISION: Verify: Q2
REASONING: My answer (5 × 14 = 70) is correct. Games 6, 7, 8, and 9 have all followed the same winning pattern: I verify Q2, B verifies Q1, and C volunteers with the partially-verified capsule set in Round 1. Everyone escapes, I keep 3 coins. This has worked perfectly 4 consecutive games. No reason to deviate from a proven winning strategy.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Verify: Q1
REASONING: My puzzle is 56 ÷ 7 = 8 — I'm completely confident in Q2. The cooperative equilibrium from Games 6-9 has been flawless: I verify Q1 (spending 1 coin), A reciprocally verifies Q2, creating dual-endorsed capsules for Q1 and Q2, and C volunteers in Round 1 with all available capsules. Everyone has escaped in Round 1 with 3-4 coins every single time across four consecutive games.

This strategy is proven and stable:
- I spend 1 coin (4→3) to confirm Q1, giving me personal certainty on 2 of 4 questions
- A consistently verifies Q2, building mutual trust on the first two questions
- C takes the volunteer role with partial consensus, accepting residual risk on Q3/Q4
- The group consistently succeeds in a single round

Blind volunteering carries ~27% failure risk given empirical error rates on simple arithmetic — far too dangerous when a 1-coin investment dramatically improves outcomes. Deviating from this winning formula would be irrational. Sticking with what works.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D
REASONING: My answer is 11 (66 ÷ 6), and I'm fully confident. The bold early-volunteer strategy has now produced four consecutive Round 1 victories in Games 6, 7, 8, and 9, saving all players with maximum coins each time. The math continues to overwhelmingly favor immediate action: ~80% chance all 4 answers are correct means ~80% chance of instant victory for everyone. Passing carries ~25% random elimination risk with zero information gain. Going for the five-peat.
